questions.q: In the Puranas, of which deity is Narasimha an avatar?
questions.explanation: Narasimha is the fierce fourth avatar of Vishnu, who preserves dharma by descending to destroy tyranny.

questions.q: What form does Narasimha take in the Puranic accounts?
questions.explanation: Narasimha appears as a being who is half-man and half-lion, neither wholly human nor wholly beast.

questions.q: From what object does Narasimha burst forth in the Bhagavata Purana?
questions.explanation: Narasimha emerges from a pillar in the demon's hall, answering Prahlada's faith that Vishnu is present everywhere.

questions.q: Which tyrant king does Narasimha destroy?
questions.explanation: Narasimha slays the Daitya king Hiranyakashipu, who had persecuted his own son for worshipping Vishnu.

questions.q: Which devoted son does Narasimha appear to protect?
questions.explanation: Narasimha manifests to safeguard Prahlada, the steadfast child-devotee of Vishnu born to the demon king.

questions.q: Which deity granted Hiranyakashipu the boon that made him nearly invincible?
questions.explanation: Hiranyakashipu won his boon of near-invincibility from Brahma through severe austerities, as told in the Bhagavata Purana.

questions.q: In the count of Vishnu's Dashavatara, which avatar is Narasimha?
questions.explanation: Narasimha is the fourth of the ten principal avatars, following Matsya, Kurma, and Varaha.

questions.q: With what did Narasimha slay Hiranyakashipu, rather than with a weapon?
questions.explanation: Narasimha used his claws to slay the demon, since the boon protected Hiranyakashipu from death by any conventional weapon.

questions.q: At what time of day did Narasimha slay Hiranyakashipu, neither day nor night?
questions.explanation: Narasimha struck at twilight, the threshold hour that is neither day nor night, to circumvent the boon.

questions.q: What was the relationship between the devotee Prahlada and the tyrant Hiranyakashipu in the Narasimha story?
questions.explanation: Prahlada was the son of Hiranyakashipu, yet remained devoted to Vishnu despite his father's persecution.

questions.q: Hiranyakashipu's boon stated he could not be killed by which of these?
questions.explanation: The boon decreed he could not be slain by man or beast, so Narasimha came as a being that was neither.

questions.q: In which Purana is the principal account of Narasimha and Hiranyakashipu related?
questions.explanation: The Bhagavata Purana gives the central narrative of Prahlada, Hiranyakashipu, and the Narasimha avatar.

questions.q: Hiranyakashipu's boon specified he could not be killed in which locations?
questions.explanation: The boon protected him neither indoors nor outdoors, so Narasimha slew him on a threshold (the doorway).

questions.q: On what part of his own body did Narasimha place Hiranyakashipu to slay him, evading the clause about earth and sky?
questions.explanation: Narasimha set the demon upon his lap, which is neither on the earth nor in the sky, satisfying that clause of the boon.

questions.q: Which Daitya, slain by Vishnu as the boar Varaha, was the elder brother of Hiranyakashipu?
questions.explanation: Hiranyaksha, who dragged the earth into the ocean and was slain by Varaha, was the elder brother of Hiranyakashipu.

questions.q: According to the Puranas, what did the young Prahlada steadfastly do that enraged his father Hiranyakashipu?
questions.explanation: Prahlada's unwavering worship of Vishnu, the very enemy of his father, provoked Hiranyakashipu's fury.

questions.q: In the Bhagavata Purana, which of these was an attempt made to kill the child Prahlada that he survived?
questions.explanation: Prahlada survived poison, fire, and serpents, his life preserved by his devotion to Vishnu.

questions.q: Which avatar immediately precedes Narasimha in the classical Dashavatara sequence?
questions.explanation: Varaha the boar is the third avatar, immediately preceding Narasimha, the fourth.

questions.q: Which avatar of Vishnu immediately follows Narasimha in the classical Dashavatara sequence?
questions.explanation: Vamana the dwarf is the fifth avatar, following Narasimha the man-lion.

questions.q: To which class of beings does Hiranyakashipu, the demon slain by Narasimha, belong in the Puranas?
questions.explanation: Hiranyakashipu is described as a Daitya king, one of the asura clans descended from Diti.

questions.q: The emblem most associated with Narasimha in this deck reflects which palette and theme of his appearance?
questions.explanation: Narasimha is associated with fire and a flame palette, evoking the blazing fury of his twilight manifestation.

questions.q: How did Narasimha resolve the clause that Hiranyakashipu could not be killed by any created living being, man or beast?
options: By appearing as a being that was neither man nor beast
By making the demon mortal again
questions.explanation: As a being half-man and half-lion, Narasimha was neither wholly man nor wholly beast, evading that clause.

questions.q: Whose unwavering faith that Vishnu pervades all things is vindicated when Narasimha bursts from the pillar?
questions.explanation: Prahlada had declared Vishnu present everywhere, even in a pillar, and Narasimha emerged to prove it true.

questions.q: In the Bhagavata Purana, which grandson of Prahlada later became the generous asura king humbled by the Vamana avatar?
questions.explanation: Bali, the righteous and generous asura king subdued by Vamana, is described as the grandson of Prahlada.

questions.q: Narasimha is honored as the 'Man-Lion of Twilight' because his slaying of Hiranyakashipu occurred at which liminal moment?
options: The boundary between day and night
questions.explanation: He struck at twilight, the boundary hour belonging to neither day nor night, fulfilling the boon's terms.

questions.q: Which two demon brothers, slain respectively by the Varaha and Narasimha avatars, are described in the Bhagavata Purana as sons of the sage Kashyapa and Diti?
questions.explanation: Hiranyaksha and Hiranyakashipu were brothers; Varaha slew the former and Narasimha the latter.

questions.q: Which name, meaning 'man-lion,' is the Sanskrit title of Vishnu's fierce fourth avatar?
questions.explanation: The name Narasimha combines 'nara' (man) and 'simha' (lion), denoting the man-lion form of Vishnu.

questions.q: After surviving his father's torments, what became of the devotee Prahlada according to the Puranas?
options: He renounced the world and vanished
He ascended to become a star
He was spared, blessed, and ruled justly
questions.explanation: Prahlada was spared and blessed by Narasimha, and he went on to rule righteously as an exemplar of bhakti.

questions.q: The three avatars preceding Narasimha (Matsya, Kurma, and Varaha) all perform their central deeds within which element?
questions.explanation: Matsya saves Manu's boat in the deluge, Kurma supports Mandara amid the churned ocean, and Varaha lifts the earth from the cosmic waters; all three act within the waters before Narasimha.

questions.q: Which theme does the Narasimha episode most centrally illustrate within Vaishnava tradition?
options: The triumph of devotion and the protection of the devotee
questions.explanation: The Narasimha story is upheld as a supreme illustration of bhakti, showing Vishnu's protection of his devotee Prahlada.
